risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "An allocation list was prepared using computer-generated random numbers and a block size of six." Comment: This explicitly describes a proper random method (computer-generated random numbers) with block randomization, which supports truly random sequence generation. The additional stratification by HIV status is also consistent with a structured randomization process rather than a predictable allocation rule.

_evidence.method_quote: An allocation list was prepared using computer-generated random numbers and a block size of six.
_evidence.method_summary: Allocation sequence was generated using computer-generated random numbers with blocks of six.
context_clues: Assignment to the three treatment arms was done separately for three cohorts of children stratified by HIV status of child and mother

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "An allocation list was prepared using computer-generated random numbers and a block size of six. The manufacturer prepared numbered packs of tablets corresponding to the allocation list." Comment: The study explicitly states that treatment packs were numbered and prepared by the manufacturer to correspond to the allocation list, which indicates a concealment mechanism that would prevent recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment before enrollment.

_evidence.concealment_quote: An allocation list was prepared using computer-generated random numbers and a block size of six. The manufacturer prepared numbered packs of tablets corresponding to the allocation list.
_evidence.note: Sequence generation is described, and allocation concealment is addressed through manufacturer-prepared numbered packs corresponding to the allocation list.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The study was a randomized, controlled double-blinded community-based trial... Investigators, study staff and participants were blind to the treatment assignments." Comment: The article explicitly states that both participants and study staff were blinded, and the supplements were made identical in appearance/taste, making broken blinding unlikely. Although some outcomes were subjective (e.g. diarrhoea incidence), adequate blinding was reported, so performance bias is judged low.

_evidence.blinding_quote: The study was a randomized, controlled double-blinded community-based trial of prophylactic zinc supplements, or multiple micronutrient supplements including zinc, given to three cohorts of children starting at age 6 months and continuing until age 24 months. ... Investigators, study staff and participants were blind to the treatment assignments.
_evidence.blinding_method: The manufacturer prepared numbered packs of tablets corresponding to the allocation list. All supplements were packaged in identical blister packs of seven tablets. All trial supplement tablets had the same color, form, texture and taste.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Investigators, study staff and participants were blind to the treatment assignments." Comment: Outcome assessment was performed by trained study nurses/field workers for anthropometry, hemoglobin testing, and weekly collection of diarrhoea information, and the trial explicitly states blinding of investigators, staff, and participants. The outcomes were largely objective (anthropometry, hemoglobin), and although diarrhoea was patient-reported, participants were blinded, so knowledge of allocation was unlikely to bias outcome measurement.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: Investigators, study staff and participants were blind to the treatment assignments.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Information on length Z-scores at two or more occasions was available for 317 (85%) of 373 children enrolled in the study (Figure 1)... There was no significant difference between treatment arms in the proportion of children who were lost to follow-up (including withdrawal or death)." Comment: The primary growth analysis used repeated-measures mixed effects regression with maximum likelihood estimation, and attrition was moderate overall (56/124, 52/123, 55/126 lost; about 15% total loss with only 2.4 percentage points differential across arms), with no meaningful arm imbalance reported. Although hemoglobin analyses were complete-case at 12 months, the key outcome data used for analysis were handled with mixed models and the reported loss is not at a level or imbalance likely to bias the comparison.

per_arm_flow.reasons: Information on length Z-scores at two or more occasions was available for 317 (85%) of 373 children enrolled in the study (Figure 1). Baseline hemoglobin measurements were available for 254 (68%) of the 373 children, baseline and 12 month hemoglobin measurements were available for 210 children. There was no significant difference between treatment arms in the proportion of children who were lost to follow-up (including withdrawal or death).

Information on length Z-scores at two or more occasions was available for 317 (85%) of 373 children enrolled in the study (Figure 1). Baseline hemoglobin measurements were available for 254 (68%) of the 373 children, baseline and 12 month hemoglobin measurements were available for 210 children. There was no significant difference between treatment arms in the proportion of children who were lost to follow-up (including withdrawal or death).

Information on length Z-scores at two or more occasions was available for 317 (85%) of 373 children enrolled in the study (Figure 1). Baseline hemoglobin measurements were available for 254 (68%) of the 373 children, baseline and 12 month hemoglobin measurements were available for 210 children. There was no significant difference between treatment arms in the proportion of children who were lost to follow-up (including withdrawal or death).

_evidence.missing_data_method: mixed-effects regression models / maximum likelihood estimation for growth; complete-case for anemia/hemoglobin analyses
_evidence.notes: Primary growth analysis used repeated-measures mixed effects regression with maximum likelihood estimation and included children with length Z-scores at two or more occasions. Hemoglobin/anemia analyses were confined to subjects with hemoglobin available at baseline and 12 months.
